text: 3 pts and GD 0 might not be enough. Right now, I see only 3 groups with potential 3rd places with less than 4 pts. We might have more than that or less depending on Mexico and Germany wanting to still get a results even if it doesn’t matter. Group C is the only group right now where it’s possible to have 3rd placed team with 3 pts in Scotland - simply because Brazil needs the win badly, so they’ll try
label: analysis
notes: Explains why Uruguay may need more than three points to qualify through the third-place table.

text: Spain will probably play for a tie. Cape Verde needs a big win for Spain to not be first place. If that’s the case Uruguay still have a good chance to qualify with 3 points and 0 GD
label: analysis

text: If Spain is smart, they play for a tie. No way Cape Verde destroys Saudi Arabia to get the sufficient GD to take first place. So, by playing for a tie, they can kick out Uruguay right away.
label: analysis
notes: Explains a possible strategic incentive for Spain in the final match.

text: The ref gave freekick at 98min 06sec. He then ended the match at 98m 23s. At the time of ending, Cape Verde had 2 players near the ball and 1 was walking slowly towards the 16m. The rest of Cape Verde players where quite far back and walking very slowly upwards from midfield.. they did not exactly rush upwards for a final chance. And it was over added time already. So can't blame the ref to much for ending it there. Would be a nice story to get a 2-3 at the end tho.
label: analysis
notes: Uses the timing and player positioning to explain the referee ending the match before the free kick.

text: Cape verde are very good no doubt but im curious to see how they fare against Saudi. Spain abd Uruguay are, in some ways, easy to plan for. You absorb pressure and hit them on the break when they inevitably start acting like you're beneath them. Saudi wont do that, itll be a more open game
label: analysis
notes: Explains why Cape Verde's match against Saudi Arabia may require a different tactical approach.

text: There's some truth in what you say, but then again, Uruguay's first goal had a lot of luck in it as well. A header that hits the post that just happens to luckily fall to an Uruguay player. The second goal was well worked by Uruguay but Cape Verde could have played them offside there. Cape Verde had other good chances that they didn't take. Of course, Uruguay probably had even more chances than Cape Verde did, but I think a draw is deserved. This wasn't a park the bus for 85 minutes and knick two lucky counter attacks in my opinion. There was substance and fight in what Cape Verde did and they clearly caused Uruguay a whole lot of problems.
label: analysis
notes: Weighs both teams' chances and explains why Cape Verde deserved the draw.

text: I think people might underestimate them too much assuming it's all low-blocks and parking the bus. But they genuinely have a great tactical approach and the physicality and technical expertise to challenge teams of the quality of Uruguay and Spain. It's not a fluke.
label: analysis
notes: Explains why Cape Verde's results are based on tactical and technical quality rather than luck.

text: I get the disappointment by Uruguay fans but you have to realise that Uruguay only has a population of around 3 million people, and yes literally probably 8 or 9/10 Uruguayans that play sports play football for most part but that doesn’t mean that you’re gonna continue to produce great players like Suarez, Cavani, Forlan, Godin and Valverde year in and year out
label: analysis
notes: Explains Uruguay's limits through its small player pool and difficulty replacing elite generations.
